subject: English
chapter: Poems – Dust of Snow & Fire and Ice
title: Extended Reading Passages with Questions – Dust of Snow & Fire and Ice
instructions: Read each passage carefully. Answer the questions using information only from the given paragraph.

passages.paragraph: The way a crow / Shook down on me / The dust of snow / From a hemlock tree. The poet was in a depressive or somber mood, standing under a tree that is usually associated with poison and gloom. However, a simple movement by a common bird changes the physical environment around him.
questions: What are the three natural elements mentioned in this passage?
What action does the crow perform?
What is a 'hemlock tree' usually associated with?
What was the poet's initial mood before the snow fell?
Identify the word that refers to fine particles of snow.

passages.paragraph: Has given my heart / A change of mood / And saved some part / Of a day I had rued. The small, almost insignificant act of snow falling on the poet had a profound internal effect. It stopped the cycle of regret that had dominated his day and provided a moment of unexpected grace.
questions: What part of the poet's being experienced a change?
What happened to the poet's mood?
What does the word 'rued' imply about the poet's day?
How much of the day was affected by this event?
What is the 'saving' force in this paragraph?

passages.paragraph: In 'Dust of Snow', Frost uses nontraditional symbols of nature. Instead of a beautiful nightingale or a majestic oak, he chooses a crow and a hemlock tree. These are symbols often linked to death or sadness, yet in this poem, they become the instruments of healing and positive transformation.
questions: Which two nontraditional symbols does Frost use?
What birds are usually used in poetry instead of a crow?
What do a crow and hemlock usually symbolize?
What role do these 'dark' symbols play in the poet's life here?
What is the main idea of this specific analysis?

passages.paragraph: Some say the world will end in fire, / Some say in ice. The poet introduces two popular theories about the apocalypse. These elements are not just physical forces of nature but represent the extreme ends of human emotion that have the power to destroy civilization as we know it.
questions: What are the two theories about how the world will end?
According to the text, what do fire and ice represent besides physical elements?
Who is debating the end of the world in the first two lines?
What is the ultimate fate of the world mentioned here?
Identify a word that refers to the conclusion of everything.

passages.paragraph: From what I’ve tasted of desire / I hold with those who favor fire. The speaker reflects on his own personal experiences with human passion and intense longing. Based on this 'taste' of emotion, he concludes that 'fire'—or uncontrolled desire—is a very likely cause for total destruction.
questions: What human emotion does the poet link to 'fire'?
What has the poet 'tasted' according to the text?
Whom does the poet agree with ('hold with')?
Why does the poet favor the 'fire' theory?
What does the word 'desire' suggest in this context?

passages.paragraph: But if it had to perish twice, / I think I know enough of hate / To say that for destruction ice / Is also great / And would suffice. The poet shifts his focus to a different kind of ending. He suggests that if a second destruction were necessary, the cold, silent force of hatred would be just as effective as fire.
questions: What does the word 'perish' mean in this context?
What emotion is compared to 'ice'?
How many times might the world end according to this hypothetical scenario?
Does the poet believe ice is weak or strong for destruction?
What does the word 'suffice' signify at the end of the passage?

passages.paragraph: Fire represents the 'hot' emotions: anger, greed, lust, and unbridled ambition. It is a force that consumes everything in its path rapidly. Ice, on the other hand, represents the 'cold' emotions: indifference, rigidity, and deep-seated hatred. It destroys by numbing and isolating, rather than burning.
questions: List two 'hot' emotions represented by fire.
How does fire destroy compared to ice?
What does 'ice' represent in terms of human interaction?
What is 'rigidity' in the context of this passage?
Contrast the two methods of destruction mentioned here.

passages.paragraph: The brevity of these poems is a signature of Robert Frost’s style. In 'Dust of Snow', the short lines mirror the quick, sudden fall of snow. In 'Fire and Ice', the concise structure reflects the stark, inevitable nature of the end of the world. Both poems use a minimal number of words to convey deep philosophical truths.
questions: What is a signature of Frost's style mentioned here?
What do the short lines in 'Dust of Snow' mirror?
What does the structure of 'Fire and Ice' reflect?
How would you describe the vocabulary used in these poems?
What is the purpose of using a 'minimal number of words'?

passages.paragraph: The 'Dust of Snow' is a poem of hope found in the mundane. It suggests that nature is not always a grand, terrifying force but can be a gentle healer. Even a 'crow' and a 'poisonous tree' can collaborate to save a human soul from a day spent in sorrow.
questions: What is the central theme of 'Dust of Snow' as per this passage?
Where is hope found in the poem?
How is nature depicted differently than a 'terrifying force'?
Which two elements 'collaborate' to help the poet?
What is the poet being saved from?

passages.paragraph: Ultimately, both poems warn us about the power of our internal state. Whether it is a bad mood that needs a 'change' or a world-ending 'hate,' Frost illustrates that the human heart and the natural world are deeply connected. Our emotions have the power to either ruin our day or end our world.
questions: What do both poems warn the reader about?
What two things are 'deeply connected' according to Frost?
What can a 'bad mood' require according to the text?
What is the dual power of human emotions mentioned in the final sentence?
Summarize the main lesson of this paragraph.
